name: Horticulture Essentials
description: A foundational baseline practice defining the essential alphas, competencies, and activity spaces for professional horticulture across biological systems, production operations, and professional practice.
authors: Claude Code (AI-assisted methodology translation)

focuses.name: Biological Systems
focuses.description: Living plant organisms, their growth environments, and ecological interactions governing health, development, and resilience.

focuses.name: Production Operations
focuses.description: Physical infrastructure, cultivation methods, technological systems, and resource management enabling controlled and efficient plant production.

focuses.name: Professional Practice
focuses.description: Human competencies, organizational management, regulatory compliance, and business operations required to sustain horticultural enterprises.

narrativeTypes.name: Seasonal Progression
narrativeTypes.description: A cyclical narrative structure reflecting the annual rhythm of horticultural operations in temperate climates, emphasizing preparation, growth, harvest, and renewal. Use for describing horticultural practices, workflows, or plant life cycles that follow seasonal patterns; useful for estate management, crop production schedules, and conservation projects.

narrativeElements.name: Spring (Preparation & Establishment)
narrativeElements.description: The season of renewal when soil is prepared, seeds sown, and plants establish.
narrativeElements.howToUse: Describe planning, ground preparation, planting, and early growth activities that set the foundation for the season.

narrativeElements.name: Summer (Growth & Maintenance)
narrativeElements.description: The period of peak growth and active management (irrigation, pest control, deadheading).
narrativeElements.howToUse: Describe intensive care, resource application, monitoring, and responsiveness to plant and environmental dynamics.

narrativeElements.name: Autumn (Harvest & Transition)
narrativeElements.description: The season of harvest, seed collection, and preparation for dormancy.
narrativeElements.howToUse: Describe output realization, succession planning, soil amendment, and winterization activities.

narrativeElements.name: Winter (Dormancy & Planning)
narrativeElements.description: The period of reduced biological activity and strategic planning for the next cycle.
narrativeElements.howToUse: Describe structural pruning, infrastructure maintenance, design work, and strategic reflection.

narrativeTypes.name: STAR
narrativeTypes.description: A universal problem-solving narrative structure that describes a challenge, the required work, the approach taken, and the outcome achieved. Use for documenting IPM interventions, troubleshooting technical problems, explaining competency demonstrations, or reporting project outcomes.

narrativeElements.name: Situation
narrativeElements.description: The context or challenge requiring attention.
narrativeElements.howToUse: Set the scene with relevant background (e.g., 'Aphid population exceeded action threshold in greenhouse tomatoes').

narrativeElements.name: Task
narrativeElements.howToUse: Define what needed to be accomplished (e.g., 'Reduce aphid population below threshold without harming beneficial insects').

narrativeElements.name: Action
narrativeElements.description: The steps taken or approach employed.
narrativeElements.howToUse: Describe the method, tools, or tactics used (e.g., 'Released predatory midges; monitored population; adjusted ventilation to deter aphids').

narrativeElements.name: Result
narrativeElements.howToUse: Report measurable results and lessons learned (e.g., 'Aphid population dropped 90% within 2 weeks; beneficial midge population established; no chemical intervention needed').

narrativeTypes.name: Hero's Journey
narrativeTypes.description: A universal narrative of transformation through challenge, learning, and growth; applicable to professional development, innovation adoption, or ecosystem restoration. Use for describing competency development pathways, organizational change journeys, or long-term restoration projects where transformation is central.

narrativeElements.name: Ordinary World
narrativeElements.howToUse: Describe baseline conditions, existing practices, or initial competency levels.

narrativeElements.name: Call to Adventure
narrativeElements.description: The catalyst or need for change.
narrativeElements.howToUse: Identify the driver (new regulation, market shift, environmental crisis, career aspiration).

narrativeElements.name: Challenges & Trials
narrativeElements.howToUse: Document learning experiences, failures overcome, and capabilities built.

narrativeElements.name: Transformation
narrativeElements.description: The achieved change in state, capability, or understanding.
narrativeElements.howToUse: Describe the new normal (certified professional, regenerative system, resilient ecosystem).

narrativeElements.name: Return with Knowledge
narrativeElements.howToUse: Explain how the journey benefits others (mentoring, best practice sharing, thought leadership).

narrativeTypes.name: Three-Act Structure
narrativeTypes.description: A universal narrative structure that moves from problem identification through solution implementation to impact realization. Use for business cases, grant proposals, innovation stories, or explaining how horticultural interventions address specific challenges.

narrativeElements.name: Act I: Problem (Setup)
narrativeElements.description: The challenge, gap, or opportunity requiring attention.
narrativeElements.howToUse: Establish stakes, quantify the problem, explain why it matters (e.g., 'Water scarcity threatens crop viability; current irrigation wastes 40% of supply').

narrativeElements.name: Act II: Solution (Confrontation)
narrativeElements.description: The intervention, innovation, or approach employed.
narrativeElements.howToUse: Describe the method, technology, or strategy (e.g., 'Installed soil moisture sensors and variable-rate drip irrigation with predictive scheduling').

narrativeElements.name: Act III: Impact (Resolution)
narrativeElements.description: The measurable outcomes and broader implications.
narrativeElements.howToUse: Report results, ROI, and lessons learned (e.g., 'Water use reduced 60%, yield increased 15%, system paid for itself in 18 months; now expanding to other sites').

competencies.name: Plant Science Knowledge
competencies.description: Understanding of plant physiology, taxonomy, genetics, and ecology required to diagnose problems, select appropriate species/cultivars, and optimize growing conditions.

levels.name: Basic
levels.description: Recognize common plant families using visual characteristics; understand basic plant anatomy (roots, stems, leaves, flowers, seeds); awareness of photosynthesis, respiration, transpiration processes.

levels.name: Applies
levels.description: Identify plants using scientific nomenclature (genus, species, cultivar); diagnose common nutrient deficiencies and environmental stress symptoms; understand pollination biology and phenological stages.

levels.name: Masters
levels.description: Select cultivars based on climate adaptation, disease resistance, and performance targets; interpret complex plant responses to environmental manipulation (photoperiod, vernalization, stress); design propagation protocols for diverse species and methods.

levels.name: Adapts
levels.description: Develop novel cultivation strategies for emerging crops or extreme environments; integrate genetic knowledge to inform breeding or selection programs; solve complex diagnostic challenges (multiple stressors, rare diseases).

levels.name: Innovating
levels.description: Contribute to scientific understanding of plant-environment interactions through research; develop new cultivars through breeding, selection, or biotechnology; publish peer-reviewed research or lead industry workshops on plant science.

competencies.name: Growing Media Management
competencies.description: Expertise in soil science, substrate formulation, rhizosphere dynamics, and nutrient chemistry to create and maintain optimal root environments.

levels.name: Basic
levels.description: Distinguish between soil types (sand, silt, clay) based on texture; understand the concept of pH and its effect on nutrient availability; recognize signs of poor drainage or waterlogging.

levels.name: Applies
levels.description: Conduct soil/substrate testing using field kits or interpret laboratory results; amend soils based on test results (liming, gypsum, organic matter); formulate basic peat-free growing mixes (coco coir, compost, perlite).

levels.name: Masters
levels.description: Design substrate recipes optimized for specific crops and production systems; manage fertility in soilless systems (EC/pH control, nutrient solution formulation); understand cation exchange capacity and nutrient retention dynamics.

levels.name: Adapts
levels.description: Troubleshoot complex substrate-related problems (salt accumulation, micronutrient deficiencies, pathogen buildup); integrate organic amendments to support beneficial microbial communities; design closed-loop nutrient recovery systems.

levels.name: Innovating
levels.description: Research novel substrate materials from waste streams (biochar, mushroom compost, agricultural by-products); develop proprietary substrate formulations for competitive advantage; contribute to scientific literature on rhizosphere ecology and nutrient dynamics.

competencies.name: Mechanical Operations
competencies.description: Proficiency in operating, maintaining, and troubleshooting horticultural machinery, hand tools, and construction equipment.

levels.name: Basic
levels.description: Safely operate hand tools (spades, rakes, pruners, loppers, saws); perform basic tool maintenance (cleaning, sharpening, oiling); understand safety protocols (PPE, manual handling, hazard awareness).

levels.name: Applies
levels.description: Operate powered equipment with certification (mowers, brush-cutters, chainsaws: NPTC/LANTRA); conduct pre-operational checks and minor repairs (changing blades, adjusting tension); use abrasive wheel equipment for cutting hard materials (paving, stone).

levels.name: Masters
levels.description: Operate complex machinery (tractors with implements, precision sprayers, excavators); diagnose mechanical faults and perform field repairs; calibrate equipment for accurate application rates.

levels.name: Adapts
levels.description: Modify or fabricate tools/equipment for specialized applications; train others in safe machinery operation and maintenance; manage machinery fleets (procurement, scheduling, lifecycle cost optimization).

levels.name: Innovating
levels.description: Collaborate with manufacturers to develop improved equipment designs; implement autonomous machinery systems (robotic mowers, driverless sprayers); conduct research on ergonomic tool design or operational efficiency.

competencies.name: Digital Technology
competencies.description: Capability to deploy, configure, operate, and interpret data from sensors, automation systems, and analytics platforms.

levels.name: Basic
levels.description: Use basic digital tools (smartphones, tablets, computers) for record-keeping; understand the concept of sensors measuring environmental parameters; awareness of IoT, automation, and AI in horticulture.

levels.name: Applies
levels.description: Program automated irrigation controllers with time-based or sensor-triggered schedules; read and interpret sensor data dashboards (temperature, humidity, soil moisture); troubleshoot basic connectivity issues (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, cellular).

levels.name: Masters
levels.description: Configure integrated climate control systems (HVAC, CO2, lighting automation); use GIS/GPS for spatial mapping and variable-rate application planning; implement data pipelines (sensor → database → dashboard → alert).

levels.name: Adapts
levels.description: Deploy predictive analytics (machine learning models for irrigation forecasting, yield prediction); integrate disparate systems through APIs, middleware, or custom scripting; design complex automation logic for multi-stage workflows.

levels.name: Innovating
levels.description: Develop proprietary algorithms for resource optimization or crop modeling; contribute to open-source precision agriculture platforms or research projects; research novel sensor technologies, edge computing, or AI applications.

competencies.name: Plant Protection Expertise
competencies.description: Knowledge and skills for integrated pest management, including pest/disease identification, biological controls, and safe pesticide application.

levels.name: Basic
levels.description: Recognize common pest groups (aphids, caterpillars, fungal diseases, weeds); understand IPM principles (prevention, monitoring, thresholds, least-risk controls); awareness of legal requirements for pesticide use and safety.

levels.name: Applies
levels.description: Identify specific pest/disease species using field guides or reference materials; conduct systematic scouting and record pest counts using monitoring protocols; apply pesticides safely with appropriate certification (PA1/PA6 in UK).

levels.name: Masters
levels.description: Design IPM programs tailored to specific crops, growing conditions, and risk profiles; deploy biological controls (predatory insects, entomopathogenic nematodes, microbial pesticides); interpret pest forecasting models (degree-days, population dynamics, epidemiology).

levels.name: Adapts
levels.description: Manage resistance development through chemical rotation, mode-of-action diversity, and refuge strategies; integrate habitat manipulation to support beneficial organisms; troubleshoot IPM failures and adapt strategies to novel pests or changing conditions.

levels.name: Innovating
levels.description: Conduct research on novel biocontrol agents, IPM tactics, or resistance mechanisms; develop site-specific pest risk models or decision support systems; publish guidance on emerging pest threats, invasive species, or resistance management.

competencies.name: Environmental Stewardship
competencies.description: Commitment to sustainable practices, biodiversity conservation, carbon management, circular economy principles, and regenerative outcomes.

levels.name: Basic
levels.description: Awareness of environmental regulations (water quality, pesticide drift, waste disposal); understanding of basic ecology (food webs, pollination, nutrient cycles); recognize the importance of habitat conservation and resource efficiency.

levels.name: Applies
levels.description: Implement best management practices (BMPs) to reduce nutrient runoff, erosion, or pollution; create wildlife-friendly habitats (insect hotels, hedgerows, ponds, meadows); track and report environmental metrics (water use, waste generation, energy consumption).

levels.name: Masters
levels.description: Design operations for net-zero or net-positive environmental outcomes; integrate renewable energy (solar, wind, biogas) into production systems; develop circular economy strategies (compost, substrate recycling, waste valorization).

levels.name: Adapts
levels.description: Conduct life cycle assessments (LCA) to quantify environmental footprints and identify hotspots; lead carbon sequestration initiatives (biochar, agroforestry, soil organic matter building); navigate complex regulatory frameworks (carbon credits, biodiversity net gain, environmental permitting).

levels.name: Innovating
levels.description: Research regenerative horticulture practices or ecosystem restoration techniques; advocate for policy changes to support sustainable agriculture and climate action; develop industry standards for environmental performance or circular economy.

competencies.name: Business & Project Management
competencies.description: Ability to plan, budget, coordinate, and deliver horticultural projects or operations on time, within budget, and to client specifications.

levels.name: Basic
levels.description: Understand project scope, deliverables, and constraints; follow task instructions and report progress accurately; awareness of budget constraints and cost implications.

levels.name: Applies
levels.description: Develop basic project plans (task lists, timelines, resource assignments); track labor hours, material costs, and budget variances; communicate effectively with clients, suppliers, and team members.

levels.name: Masters
levels.description: Manage complex projects with multiple stakeholders, dependencies, and risks; prepare detailed budgets with contingency planning and cost-benefit analysis; negotiate contracts, manage procurement, and coordinate subcontractors.

levels.name: Adapts
levels.description: Oversee portfolio of concurrent projects with resource leveling and prioritization; implement project management software (Gantt charts, critical path, earned value); conduct post-project reviews and continuous improvement initiatives.

levels.name: Innovating
levels.description: Lead strategic business development (new markets, partnerships, service offerings); develop proprietary project management methodologies or tools; mentor others in business and project management; contribute to professional standards.

competencies.name: Professional Practice
competencies.description: Interpersonal, ethical, communication, and teamwork skills required for effective collaboration, client relations, and professional conduct.

levels.name: Basic
levels.description: Demonstrate reliability, punctuality, and positive attitude; follow instructions accurately and work safely; communicate clearly with team members and supervisors.

levels.name: Applies
levels.description: Provide excellent customer service (active listening, responsiveness, problem-solving); maintain confidentiality and discretion (especially in private estates or sensitive contexts); collaborate effectively in multi-disciplinary teams (horticulturists, engineers, ecologists).

levels.name: Masters
levels.description: Lead teams, manage volunteers, and coordinate crew activities; negotiate with clients, stakeholders, and regulators; present complex technical information to non-specialist audiences (reports, workshops, public talks).

levels.name: Adapts
levels.description: Resolve conflicts and manage difficult interpersonal situations; uphold professional ethics in challenging contexts (competing interests, resource constraints); represent the organization at industry events, public forums, or media engagements.

levels.name: Innovating
levels.description: Contribute to professional standards development (RHS, Landscape Institute, industry associations); mentor early-career professionals through formal programs or apprenticeships; advocate for the horticultural profession in policy, education, or public discourse.

narrativeContexts.context: Horticulture has historically operated through fragmented knowledge domains—plant science, engineering, business—with limited integration. Practitioners learned through apprenticeship and experience, facing environmental unpredictability, pest pressure, resource scarcity, and market volatility. Traditional methods often degraded ecosystems (soil depletion, water pollution, biodiversity loss) while failing to achieve economic viability for many operations.

Modern challenges demand a unified framework: climate change intensifies weather extremes; consumers demand sustainable production; technological advances (CEA, IoT, AI) create new opportunities; regulatory requirements tighten; global competition forces innovation. The 'Horticulture Essentials' baseline emerges as a comprehensive ontology integrating biological systems, production operations, and professional practice—a common language for the profession.

Mastering plant physiology while deploying precision agriculture technologies; balancing productivity with environmental stewardship; navigating complex regulatory frameworks; managing business viability amid volatile markets; building competency across biological, engineering, and management domains; coordinating multi-stakeholder ecosystems (growers, suppliers, regulators, customers, communities).

Horticulture evolves from a craft-based, extractive industry to a science-driven, regenerative profession. Practitioners integrate deep plant science knowledge with advanced engineering systems and strategic business management. Operations achieve triple-bottom-line success (economic viability, environmental regeneration, social contribution). The profession attracts talent, earns public respect, and becomes recognized as essential to food security, climate mitigation, and urban well-being.

Chartered professionals mentor the next generation through formal apprenticeships and continuing professional development programs. Industry leaders contribute to policy development, standard-setting, and public education. Research advances shared through peer-reviewed publications, industry conferences, and open-source platforms. The Horticulture Essentials baseline becomes the foundation for specialized extension practices (e.g., 'Vertical Farming Operations,' 'Heritage Garden Restoration,' 'Urban Greening Programs') that build upon this shared ontology.
